one_line_takeaway: A first-of-its-kind survey that taxonomizes ~46 studies using LLMs for blockchain security into five roles (auditing, transaction anomaly detection, fuzzing, contract development, community participation), with smart-contract auditing dominating and LLM-generated contracts flagged as a security risk.
key_finding: LLMs are being applied across the full lifecycle of blockchain security, with smart-contract auditing being by far the most active area (22 of the surveyed works, vs. 3 each for transaction detection and dynamic analysis, 8 for contract development, and 5 for community/governance). LLM-based tools shift auditing from static pattern matching toward context-aware, 'multimodal' inspection that can catch 'machine un-auditable' logic bugs. A notable cautionary finding drawn from the development-focused studies: LLMs readily produce syntactically correct Solidity yet frequently generate contracts with substantial security vulnerabilities, so their output requires thorough human review and careful prompt engineering.
core_contribution: A systematic literature review (survey) that maps the emerging field of applying Large Language Models to blockchain security (termed 'LLM4BS'). The paper's OWN contribution is not a new tool or experiment but (1) a thematic taxonomy that organizes ~46 surveyed studies into five functional roles for the LLM plus a miscellaneous bucket, and (2) three in-depth 'case study' walkthroughs (LLM4FUZZ, SMARTINV, BLOCKGPT) meant to illustrate the state of the art. The authors claim it is the first systematic examination of LLMs specifically at the intersection of AI and blockchain security. It explicitly does NOT propose or evaluate any new system.
threat_model: As a survey the paper defines no threat model of its own; instead it catalogs the blockchain security threats that the surveyed LLM tools aim to address. It groups these into: consensus-based attacks (51% attacks, selfish mining, eclipse attacks, long-range attacks); smart-contract exploits (reentrancy, integer overflow/underflow, unchecked external calls, gas-limit vulnerabilities); DeFi protocol vulnerabilities (flash-loan attacks, oracle manipulation, governance exploits); and auxiliary-service vulnerabilities (exchange hacks, wallet vulnerabilities, oracle manipulation). The LLM is positioned as a defensive analyst (auditor, anomaly detector, fuzzer guide), though the paper notes LLM-generated smart contracts can themselves introduce vulnerabilities.
limitations: This is a preliminary manuscript and a narrative survey, so it inherits the usual survey weaknesses: no stated search protocol, inclusion/exclusion criteria, or database queries are given, making it hard to judge coverage completeness despite the 'systematic' label. Much of the prose is descriptive and promotional rather than critically comparative; there is little cross-tool quantitative comparison because the surveyed papers use disparate datasets and metrics. Several table entries are marked 'Unknown' (e.g., ZipZap's functionality and LLM). The identified challenges (scalability, privacy, ethics, regulation) are discussed at a high level. All performance numbers belong to the cited primary works, not to any evaluation the authors themselves ran.

datasets_used: No dataset is used by the survey itself; it is a literature review. Datasets appear only inside quoted case studies: BLOCKGPT was trained on transactional data from the Ethereum blockchain (per the surveyed BLOCKGPT paper), and its reported evaluation involved 124 verified attack transactions.
quantitative_results: Taxonomy counts of surveyed studies (the survey's own tally): Smart Contract Auditing = 22 works; Block Transaction Detection = 3; Contract Dynamic Analysis = 3; Smart Contract Development = 8; Cryptocurrency Community Contributors = 5; Miscellaneous = 5
Quoted from surveyed BLOCKGPT paper (NOT the survey's own experiment): correctly ranked 49 of 124 verified attack transactions among the three most-abnormal transactions in their victim contracts
Quoted from surveyed BLOCKGPT paper: average processing throughput of 2,284 transactions per second
Three case-study works profiled: LLM4FUZZ (fuzzing, UC Berkeley, 2024), SMARTINV (program analysis, IEEE S&P/Columbia, 2024), BLOCKGPT (transaction analysis, Univ. of California, 2023)
baselines_compared: The survey performs no baseline comparison of its own. It qualitatively contrasts LLM-based approaches against traditional static/rule-based analysis and conventional (scattergun) fuzzing, citing each surveyed paper's claim of outperforming those baselines (e.g., LLM4FUZZ reportedly outperforms baseline fuzzing; BLOCKGPT vs. rule-based intrusion detection).
